Metadata.Coram: Chao Hick Tin JA; L P Thean JA; Yong Pung How CJ
Metadata.Judge: Chao Hick Tin JA; L P Thean JA; Yong Pung How CJ

Party_Details.Name: Low Hua Kin

Facts.Text: In the early 1970s, Low Hua Kin worked as a lecturer at the University of Singapore.
In 1972, while still a lecturer, Low Hua Kin, with the consent of the university, began working for Kumagai Gumi Co Ltd as a consultant and advisor.
From 1972, Low Hua Kin had a consultancy and advisory relationship with Kumagai Gumi Co Ltd, a Japanese construction corporation operating in Singapore.
In 1976, Low Hua Kin resigned from the University of Singapore and became a full-time consultant and advisor to Kumagai Gumi Co Ltd.
In 1979, with the consent of Kumagai Gumi Co Ltd, Low Hua Kin incorporated Zenecon Pte Ltd, a company under his control.
From 1979, Low Hua Kin was a director and chairman of Zenecon Pte Ltd and held controlling shares in the company.
In 1979, the other shareholders of Zenecon Pte Ltd were the wife and brother of Low Hua Kin, and Jason Lim was the company secretary.

On 25 April 1983, Kumagai Gumi Co Ltd and Zenecon Pte Ltd executed a shareholders' agreement and memorandum of understanding to form a joint venture company for construction, civil and structural engineering, and property development.
On 3 June 1983, Kumagai-Zenecon Construction Pte Ltd was incorporated as the joint venture company pursuant to the shareholders' agreement.
From incorporation on 3 June 1983, Zenecon Pte Ltd held 51% of the shares in Kumagai-Zenecon Construction Pte Ltd and Kumagai Gumi Co Ltd held 49%.

Under the joint venture arrangements effective from 3 June 1983, Kumagai Gumi Co Ltd was to provide technical expertise and working capital, while Zenecon Pte Ltd was to manage the day-to-day operations of Kumagai-Zenecon Construction Pte Ltd.
From 3 June 1983, Kumagai Gumi Co Ltd and Zenecon Pte Ltd each appointed three nominee directors to the board of Kumagai-Zenecon Construction Pte Ltd, and Low Hua Kin was appointed managing director.

In November 1984, with the agreement of Kumagai Gumi Co Ltd, Kumagai-Zenecon Construction Pte Ltd caused the incorporation of Kumagai Property Marketing Pte Ltd as a subsidiary to market housing development projects.

Soon after its incorporation, Kumagai Property Marketing Pte Ltd allotted 9,000 shares to Kumagai-Zenecon Construction Pte Ltd and 1,000 shares to Lim Thye San, the brother-in-law of Low Hua Kin and property manager of the company.
After the initial allotment, the 1,000 shares in Kumagai Property Marketing Pte Ltd held by Lim Thye San were acquired by Low Hua Kin.
From the time of its early operations, the directors of Kumagai Property Marketing Pte Ltd consisted of Low Hua Kin and his nominees, and there were no Kumagai Gumi Co Ltd nominees on its board.
From its early operations, Low Hua Kin was appointed as the representative of Kumagai-Zenecon Construction Pte Ltd at general meetings of Kumagai Property Marketing Pte Ltd.
Subsequently, Low Hua Kin began operating Kumagai Property Marketing Pte Ltd in a manner treating it as if it were wholly owned by him.

Without prior consultation with Kumagai Gumi Co Ltd, Low Hua Kin used Kumagai Property Marketing Pte Ltd to purchase shares in certain public listed companies with the objective of gaining control in those companies.

To facilitate share investments, Low Hua Kin caused the name of Kumagai Property Marketing Pte Ltd to be changed to Kumagai Investments Pte Ltd and altered its memorandum of association to include investing in shares as part of its business.
In May 1991, Kumagai Investments Pte Ltd purchased approximately 7,321,000 shares in Pacific Can Investment Holdings Ltd following directions from Low Hua Kin.

On 10 May 1991, Kumagai Investments Pte Ltd entered into a loan agreement with Arab Bank plc for a loan facility of up to US$6,000,000 to finance the purchase of Pacific Can Investment Holdings Ltd shares, with those shares charged to the bank as security.

By 10 May 1991, Kumagai Gumi Co Ltd had not been informed by Low Hua Kin or Kumagai Investments Pte Ltd of the change of name, the share purchase transactions, or the loan agreement with Arab Bank plc.
In August 1991, Kumagai Investments Pte Ltd purchased an additional 278,000 shares in Pacific Can Investment Holdings Ltd without the knowledge of Kumagai Gumi Co Ltd.
By August 1991, Kumagai Investments Pte Ltd had acquired a total of 7,599,000 shares in Pacific Can Investment Holdings Ltd at the direction of Low Hua Kin.
By late 1991, the relationship between Kumagai Gumi Co Ltd and Low Hua Kin had become strained, and disputes arose between them.
On 29 January 1992, Kumagai Gumi Co Ltd filed a petition under section 216 of the Companies Act in relation to Kumagai-Zenecon Construction Pte Ltd.

On 29 January 1992, Kumagai Gumi Co Ltd presented a petition for the winding up of Kumagai-Zenecon Construction Pte Ltd under section 254(1)(i) of the Companies Act on the basis of a breakdown in the relationship between Kumagai Gumi Co Ltd and Zenecon Pte Ltd.
On 11 February 1992, by consent order of court, Don Ho and Wee Aik Guan of Deloitte and Touche were appointed as provisional liquidators of Kumagai-Zenecon Construction Pte Ltd.
On 15 April 1992, Arab Bank plc issued a margin call to Kumagai Investments Pte Ltd requiring payment of S$220,000, which the company could not meet until a partial payment of S$30,000 was made.

On 22 April 1992, Arab Bank plc informed Kumagai Investments Pte Ltd that it intended to sell sufficient Pacific Can Investment Holdings Ltd shares to cover the margin shortfall unless payment was made by 2:30 pm that day, and accepted a partial payment of S$30,000 from the company.
By 23 April 1992, Kumagai Investments Pte Ltd had received another margin call from Arab Bank plc for S$150,000 and had a bank balance of S$3,649.37.

On 23 April 1992, the provisional liquidators of Kumagai-Zenecon Construction Pte Ltd decided that, given Kumagai Investments Pte Ltd had no income or funds to meet further margin calls or interest payments, selling the Pacific Can Investment Holdings Ltd shares en bloc was the preferred solution.

After receiving notice of the proposed en bloc sale at S$0.78 per share, Low Hua Kin, through his solicitors, stated that he agreed only to the sale of such number of shares as necessary to meet the margin call and requested the identity of the intended purchaser.
The offer to purchase all Pacific Can Investment Holdings Ltd shares at S$0.78 per share was not accepted and lapsed in or around May 1992.

Following the lapse of the S$0.78 per share offer, the provisional liquidators continued to seek the cooperation of Low Hua Kin to sell the Pacific Can Investment Holdings Ltd shares en bloc, while he responded that he did not consider a full disposal to be in the best interests of Kumagai Investments Pte Ltd and did not propose an alternative plan.
For the financial year ending 30 September 1991, Pacific Can Investment Holdings Ltd recorded poor profits, and around that time newspapers reported rumours of a boardroom tussle in the company.

On 12 June 1992, Low Hua Kin and his associates were removed as directors of Kumagai Investments Pte Ltd, and Kumagai-Zenecon Construction Pte Ltd, acting through the provisional liquidators, appointed two nominees to the board in their place.
On 22 June 1992, Kumagai Investments Pte Ltd defaulted on an interest payment amounting to $42,076.39.

By a letter dated 24 June 1992, the bank gave notice that the failure to pay the interest constituted an event of default under the loan agreement and demanded payment from KPM of the entire outstanding sum inclusive of interest amounting in total to $3,074,040.37, in default of which they said that they would exercise all their rights under the loan agreement without further reference to the company.

Accordingly, on 25 June 1992, the secretary of KPM sent to the shareholders of KPM a notice calling for an extraordinary general meeting of the company to be held on 10 July 1992 for the purpose of passing an ordinary resolution authorising the directors to sell the shares. The secretary also requested shareholders to consent to shorter notice of the meeting so that the matter could be resolved early.
On 1 July 1992, Arab Bank wrote to KPM agreeing to give the company a short period of time, until 3 July 1992, to arrange a `married deal` for the en bloc sale of the Pac Can shares.

The appellant himself, on 3 July 1992, wrote direct to KPM, in response to the secretary's request for consent to a shorter notice of the extraordinary general meeting. In that letter he raised a series of queries on the sale of the shares, namely, the identity of the proposed buyer, the proposed selling price, whether the bank had consented to the sale, and what the directors of KPM proposed to do with the proceeds of sale and surplus funds after payment of the loan to the bank. On the same day, KPM replied by fax furnishing him the replies to his queries, and by a separate fax sought his consent to the shorter notice.

On 10 July 1992, an extraordinary general meeting of the shareholders of KPM was held. K-Z was represented by Don Ho, one of the provisional liquidators. The appellant for his part did not show up. Instead, he sent Jason Lim as his proxy. At this meeting, a resolution authorising the directors to dispose of the whole or substantially the whole of the company's undertaking was passed. Jason Lim abstained from voting.
On 14 July 1992, KPM wrote to the appellant asking him to offer a price for the purchase of the shares, if he wished to purchase them.

On 16 July 1992, KPM sent another letter to the appellant repeating the above request, giving the appellant up till the end of the working day on 17 July 1992 to make an offer. A separate letter to the same effect was also sent to the appellant's solicitors.

The appellant replied through his solicitors on 17 July 1992, stating that he did not wish to make a first offer to purchase the Pac Can shares from KPM, and repeating his earlier request for a right of first refusal.

The provisional liquidators' solicitors replied on 18 July 1992 denying that the appellant had any such right of first refusal. They maintained that their objective was to obtain the best possible price for the shares.

Finally, on 21 July 1992 KPM sent the following fax to the appellant: "We have an interested party to purchase all the Kumagai Investments` shares in Pacific Can for 70 cents per share. Are you interested to pay a higher price? If so, please revert before 5 pm today."

No offer or indication of any interest from the appellant having been received by KPM by the time stated in the notice, the shares were sold on 22 July 1992 at 70 cents per share. However, it later transpired that the appellant had sent a fax to the directors of KPM dated 21 July 1992 making an offer to purchase the shares at 70.25 cents per share and further asked for 14 days to complete the sale. Don Ho did not see this letter until 23 July 1992, by which time he had already accepted the offer to sell the shares at 70 cents per share on behalf of KPM.
The net loss incurred by KPM in the sale of the Pac Can shares amounted to $2,982,517.17.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the financial loss of S$2,982,517.17 incurred by Kumagai Investments Pte Ltd on the sale of Pacific Can Investment Holdings Ltd shares, acquired using borrowed funds and directed by a managing director, is legally attributable to that director's pre-sale conduct in causing the company to acquire and hold those shares on margin financing.
Party_Details.Rule: Where a company suffers a financial loss on the disposal of assets acquired under the direction of a director, the company may seek to recover that loss from the director if there is a sufficient causal connection between the director's management decisions and the loss, and if intervening acts, such as decisions by liquidators or agents, do not break the chain of causation.
Party_Details.Application: Low Hua Kin directed the incorporation and structure of Zenecon Pte Ltd and later Kumagai Investments Pte Ltd, controlled their boards, and caused Kumagai Investments Pte Ltd to acquire 7,599,000 Pacific Can Investment Holdings Ltd shares using a US$6,000,000 loan from Arab Bank plc secured on those shares. Kumagai Investments Pte Ltd had limited cash and no independent income, leading to margin calls, interest defaults, and a demand for full repayment. The provisional liquidators, acting for Kumagai-Zenecon Construction Pte Ltd as majority shareholder, obtained shareholder approval under section 160 of the Companies Act and arranged an en bloc sale at S$0.70 per share, crystallising a loss of S$2,982,517.17. The companies' claim links this loss to the director's earlier decisions on borrowing and concentrated investment, while the director argues that the provisional liquidators' sale decisions and ultra vires or negligent conduct constitute intervening acts that break the causal chain.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court held that the director owed fiduciary duties to both companies and that his procurement of the purchase of shares on borrowed money constituted a breach of those duties. The court accepted that the investment conferred no commercial benefit on the company and served only the director's personal objectives. It held that the loss of S$2,982,517.17 was caused by the breach of duty and that the subsequent actions of the provisional liquidators did not break the chain of causation. The court rejected arguments that the provisional liquidators acted ultra vires or negligently, finding that they had statutory power to realise assets and made a reasonable commercial decision given the mounting margin calls, lack of funds, and market rumours. The court noted that the director had multiple opportunities to make a firm offer for the shares or to inject funds but declined to do so until after the sale was effectively concluded. It concluded that the director was liable to reimburse the company the sum of S$2,982,517.17.

Metadata.Coram: Chao Hick Tin JA; L P Thean JA; Yong Pung How CJ
Metadata.Judge: Chao Hick Tin JA; L P Thean JA; Yong Pung How CJ

Party_Details.Name: Kumagai-Zenecon Construction Pte Ltd (in liquidation) and Kumagai Property Marketing Pte Ltd / Kumagai Investments Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Kumagai-Zenecon Construction Pte Ltd was incorporated on 3 June 1983 as a joint venture company between Kumagai Gumi Co Ltd and Zenecon Pte Ltd.
As provided in the agreement, Zenecon held 51% of the shares in K-Z, while Kumagai held the remaining 49% of the shares.
Under the agreement, Kumagai was to supply the necessary technical expertise and working capital, while Zenecon was to assume the responsibility for the day-to-day management of the company.
Both parties to the joint venture appointed three nominee directors to the board of K-Z respectively.

In November 1984, with the agreement of Kumagai, a subsidiary of K-Z called Kumagai Property Marketing Pte Ltd (KPM) was formed for the purpose of marketing the housing development projects undertaken by K-Z.
Soon after the incorporation, K-Z was allotted 9,000 shares of KPM, and one Lim Thye San, the brother-in-law of the appellant, who was then the property manager of KPM, was allotted 1000 shares.
These 1000 shares were subsequently acquired by the appellant.
The directors of KPM consisted of the appellant and his nominees, and there were no nominees from Kumagai on the board of this company.
The appellant was appointed the representative to represent K-Z at general meetings of KPM.

Subsequently, the appellant began to run KPM as if it were wholly owned by him. Without any prior consultation with Kumagai, he proceeded to make use of the company to purchase shares in certain public listed companies with a view to gaining control in these companies. For that purpose, he caused the name of KPM to be changed to Kumagai Investments Pte Ltd and also the objects clause in the memorandum of association of KPM to be altered so as to include the investing in shares as part of its business.

On 10 May 1991, KPM entered into a loan agreement with Arab Bank plc (`Arab Bank`) under which a loan facility up to an amount of US$6m was provided by the bank to KPM, and as security for the loan the shares purchased were charged to the bank.
In May 1991, a total of some 7,321,000 shares in Pacific Can Investment Holdings Ltd (`Pac Can`) were purchased by KPM pursuant to the appellant's directions.

In August 1991, KPM bought 278,000 more shares in Pac Can, and this purchase was again made without Kumagai's knowledge. In all, a total of 7,599,000 shares in Pac Can were bought by KPM at the direction of the appellant.
Kumagai was not informed of these transactions. It only learnt of the change of name and purchase of the shares from newspaper reports.
The relationship between Kumagai and the appellant became strained and very soon various disputes between them erupted.

On 29 January 1992, Kumagai filed a petition under section 216 of the Companies Act (Cap 50, 1990 Ed) in respect of K-Z. Kumagai also presented a petition for the winding up of K-Z under s 254(1)(i) of the Companies Act on the ground that it was just and equitable to do so by reason of the breakdown in the relationship between the two shareholders, Kumagai and Zenecon.
Soon after the filing of these petitions, an order of court by consent was made on 11 February 1992 appointing Don Ho and Wee Aik Guan of Deloitte and Touche as provisional liquidators of K-Z.

On 15 April 1992, Arab Bank made a margin call on KPM requiring the company to pay up a sum of $220,000. KPM failed to meet this demand. On 22 April 1992, the bank indicated its intention to sell sufficient amount of shares to satisfy the margin shortfall, unless the shortfall was paid by 2.30pm that day. This impending crisis was averted only after the bank agreed to accept a partial payment of $30,000 from KPM.

On 22 April 1992, the bank indicated its intention to sell sufficient amount of shares to satisfy the margin shortfall, unless the shortfall was paid by 2.30pm that day. This impending crisis was averted only after the bank agreed to accept a partial payment of $30,000 from KPM.

Subsequently, another margin call was made and KPM was requested to pay up a sum of $150,000. It became clear by this time that KPM was in dire financial straits. As at 23 April 1992, its bank balance stood at a meagre sum of $3,649.37.

The provisional liquidators took the view that as KPM had no income, no potential source of income or any other funds to meet further margin calls or even the next interest payment, the best solution in the circumstances was to sell the shares en bloc.
The offer to purchase the shares at 78 cents per share was not taken up, and it lapsed.
Pac Can had also recorded dismal profits in the financial year ending 30 September 1991.

On 12 June 1992, the appellant and his associates were removed as directors of KPM. K-Z, acting through the provisional liquidators, appointed two of their nominees to the board of KPM in place of the appellant and his associates.
On 22 June 1992, KPM defaulted on an interest payment amounting to $42,076.39.

By a letter dated 24 June 1992, the bank gave notice that the failure to pay the interest constituted an event of default under the loan agreement and demanded payment from KPM of the entire outstanding sum inclusive of interest amounting in total to $3,074,040.37, in default of which they said that they would exercise all their rights under the loan agreement without further reference to the company.

Accordingly, on 25 June 1992, the secretary of KPM sent to the shareholders of KPM a notice calling for an extraordinary general meeting of the company to be held on 10 July 1992 for the purpose of passing an ordinary resolution authorising the directors to sell the shares. The secretary also requested shareholders to consent to shorter notice of the meeting so that the matter could be resolved early.
On 1 July 1992, Arab Bank wrote to KPM agreeing to give the company a short period of time, until 3 July 1992, to arrange a `married deal` for the en bloc sale of the Pac Can shares.

The appellant himself, on 3 July 1992, wrote direct to KPM, in response to the secretary's request for consent to a shorter notice of the extraordinary general meeting. In that letter he raised a series of queries on the sale of the shares, namely, the identity of the proposed buyer, the proposed selling price, whether the bank had consented to the sale, and what the directors of KPM proposed to do with the proceeds of sale and surplus funds after payment of the loan to the bank. On the same day, KPM replied by fax furnishing him the replies to his queries, and by a separate fax sought his consent to the shorter notice.

On 10 July 1992, an extraordinary general meeting of the shareholders of KPM was held. K-Z was represented by Don Ho, one of the provisional liquidators. The appellant for his part did not show up. Instead, he sent Jason Lim as his proxy. At this meeting, a resolution authorising the directors to dispose of the whole or substantially the whole of the company's undertaking was passed. Jason Lim abstained from voting.
On 14 July 1992, KPM wrote to the appellant asking him to offer a price for the purchase of the shares, if he wished to purchase them.

On 16 July 1992, KPM sent another letter to the appellant repeating the above request, giving the appellant up till the end of the working day on 17 July 1992 to make an offer. A separate letter to the same effect was also sent to the appellant's solicitors.

The appellant replied through his solicitors on 17 July 1992, stating that he did not wish to make a first offer to purchase the Pac Can shares from KPM, and repeating his earlier request for a right of first refusal.

The provisional liquidators' solicitors replied on 18 July 1992 denying that the appellant had any such right of first refusal. They maintained that their objective was to obtain the best possible price for the shares.

Finally, on 21 July 1992 KPM sent the following fax to the appellant: "We have an interested party to purchase all the Kumagai Investments` shares in Pacific Can for 70 cents per share. Are you interested to pay a higher price? If so, please revert before 5 pm today."

No offer or indication of any interest from the appellant having been received by KPM by the time stated in the notice, the shares were sold on 22 July 1992 at 70 cents per share. However, it later transpired that the appellant had sent a fax to the directors of KPM dated 21 July 1992 making an offer to purchase the shares at 70.25 cents per share and further asked for 14 days to complete the sale. Don Ho did not see this letter until 23 July 1992, by which time he had already accepted the offer to sell the shares at 70 cents per share on behalf of KPM.
The net loss incurred by KPM in the sale of the Pac Can shares amounted to $2,982,517.17.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the financial loss of S$2,982,517.17 incurred by Kumagai Investments Pte Ltd on the sale of Pacific Can Investment Holdings Ltd shares, acquired using borrowed funds and directed by a managing director, is legally attributable to that director's pre-sale conduct in causing the company to acquire and hold those shares on margin financing.
Party_Details.Rule: Where a company suffers a financial loss on the disposal of assets acquired under the direction of a director, the company may seek to recover that loss from the director if there is a sufficient causal connection between the director's management decisions and the loss, and if intervening acts, such as decisions by liquidators or agents, do not break the chain of causation.
Party_Details.Application: Low Hua Kin directed the incorporation and structure of Zenecon Pte Ltd and later Kumagai Investments Pte Ltd, controlled their boards, and caused Kumagai Investments Pte Ltd to acquire 7,599,000 Pacific Can Investment Holdings Ltd shares using a US$6,000,000 loan from Arab Bank plc secured on those shares. Kumagai Investments Pte Ltd had limited cash and no independent income, leading to margin calls, interest defaults, and a demand for full repayment. The provisional liquidators, acting for Kumagai-Zenecon Construction Pte Ltd as majority shareholder, obtained shareholder approval under section 160 of the Companies Act and arranged an en bloc sale at S$0.70 per share, crystallising a loss of S$2,982,517.17. The companies' claim links this loss to the director's earlier decisions on borrowing and concentrated investment, while the director argues that the provisional liquidators' sale decisions and ultra vires or negligent conduct constitute intervening acts that break the causal chain.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court held that the director owed fiduciary duties to both companies and that his procurement of the purchase of shares on borrowed money constituted a breach of those duties. The court accepted that the investment conferred no commercial benefit on the company and served only the director's personal objectives. It held that the loss of S$2,982,517.17 was caused by the breach of duty and that the subsequent actions of the provisional liquidators did not break the chain of causation. The court rejected arguments that the provisional liquidators acted ultra vires or negligently, finding that they had statutory power to realise assets and made a reasonable commercial decision given the mounting margin calls, lack of funds, and market rumours. The court noted that the director had multiple opportunities to make a firm offer for the shares or to inject funds but declined to do so until after the sale was effectively concluded. It concluded that the director was liable to reimburse the company the sum of S$2,982,517.17.
